narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 66% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. This prediction draws from key aspects of your background, like your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, LSAT score of 26, low class rankings in your first and third years of school (both in the bottom decile), lower-middle family income, and your status as an Asian male planning full-time studies. Compared to typical applicants—who average around a 3.21 GPA, 36.7 on the LSAT, mid-level class rankings, and higher family incomes—your profile shows weaker performance in several academic indicators that strongly correlate with bar success. The model's decision was influenced most heavily by your LSAT score, which had the biggest impact in pushing the prediction toward failure since it's well below the norm. Next in influence came your third-year class ranking, also low and contributing similarly toward the failure risk. Your first-year ranking followed closely, reinforcing that early academic struggles played a notable role. Family income level had a moderate but still pushing effect toward failure, as lower resources often link to tougher bar outcomes in the data. Your racial background as Asian edged in next, slightly tilting the prediction unfavorably compared to the mostly White dataset. Your undergraduate GPA rounded out the stronger influences, with its below-average mark adding to the concern. Less impactful were your gender as male and full-time study plans, which barely swayed the outcome. Overall, these factors together painted a picture riskier than most accepted students, who typically excel in LSAT and grades. To boost your future bar passing odds, focus on retaking the LSAT for a higher score if possible, building a stronger academic record through additional coursework or grad programs, and seeking financial support or study resources to offset income-related hurdles. With targeted preparation like bar-specific tutoring, many students in similar spots have turned things around—keep pursuing your goal persistently.
